critic_raw:
The most severe flaw is patient-level leakage through random splitting. The dataset contains approximately 5,000 scans from 1,000–1,500 unique participants in a 5-year longitudinal study, meaning each participant contributes an average of 3–5 scans at different time points. Stratified random sampling at the scan level places multiple scans from the same participant in both training and test. The model learns a participant's individual fMRI signature in training and then 'generalizes' to that same individual's later scan in test. This is identity leakage, not generalization, and any accuracy estimate is inflated.

Preprocessing—including functional connectivity matrix computation and PCA/ICA dimensionality reduction—is stated as fit on the full dataset before splitting. For fMRI, the covariance structure of connectivity matrices is computed across all participants, meaning test-participant brain activity patterns inform the low-dimensional space used to represent training participants. This is direct leakage.

The unsupervised clustering step introduces a fundamental logical circularity in the evaluation. Clusters are assigned using the full dataset (including test participants), and then a classifier is trained on cluster assignments to predict clinical labels. The cluster structure is inherently contaminated by test-set information. Even if the clustering is re-run without test data in a corrected design, the design as stated uses test-contaminated cluster assignments as features for supervised classification.

The evaluation compares 'unsupervised fMRI clustering + demographics' to 'cognitive test scores + demographics' but then trains a classifier (logistic regression or XGBoost) on top of both. The hypothesis claims unsupervised neuroimaging leads to 'more accurate classification'—but the classification step introduces a supervised component to both pipelines. The question being tested is whether fMRI-derived features (after unsupervised preprocessing) feed a better classifier than cognitive scores feed a comparable classifier. Calling this 'unsupervised classification' is misleading.
all_issues_raised: Random splitting at scan level with ~3–5 scans per participant places the same participant's scans in training and test, constituting identity leakage that inflates all accuracy estimates and is not generalization to new individuals.

Functional connectivity matrices and PCA/ICA dimensionality reduction are fit on the full dataset before splitting, so the low-dimensional brain representation for training participants is informed by test-participant activity patterns.

Cluster assignments are computed on the full dataset including test participants, and these contaminated assignments are used as classification features; this makes the cluster-based features non-blind to test-set structure.

The design labels the primary method 'unsupervised classification' but trains a supervised classifier on top of the cluster features; both pipelines use supervised models, so the comparison is between feature types (fMRI clusters vs. cognitive scores) not between supervised and unsupervised approaches.
all_issues_adjudicated: Random splitting at scan level with ~3–5 scans per participant places the same participant's scans in training and test, constituting identity leakage that inflates all accuracy estimates and is not generalization to new individuals.

Functional connectivity matrices and PCA/ICA dimensionality reduction are fit on the full dataset before splitting, so the low-dimensional brain representation for training participants is informed by test-participant activity patterns.

Cluster assignments are computed on the full dataset including test participants, and these contaminated assignments are used as classification features; this makes the cluster-based features non-blind to test-set structure.

The design labels the primary method 'unsupervised classification' but trains a supervised classifier on top of the cluster features; both pipelines use supervised models, so the comparison is between feature types (fMRI clusters vs. cognitive scores) not between supervised and unsupervised approaches.
